1:
"Simon Peter, a servant and an apostle of Jesus Christ, to them that have obtained like precious faith with us through the righteousness of God and our Saviour Jesus Christ:" The witnesses note the address and the precious faith through the righteousness of God and our Saviour — touching the deity of Christ. On "Simon Peter, a servant and an apostle of Jesus Christ," the grammatical voices note the writer. Barnes and Robertson note the double title: "Simon Peter, a servant (doulos — bondservant) and an apostle (one sent) of Jesus Christ" — Peter names himself both servant and apostle, humility joined to authority.

On "like precious faith with us," the witnesses note the shared faith. Barnes and Calvin note that the readers "have obtained (received as a gift, by lot) like precious (equally precious) faith with us (the apostles)" — the same costly, valuable faith, given to ordinary believers as to the apostles; faith is a gift, equal in worth for all who have it.

On "through the righteousness of God and our Saviour Jesus Christ," interpreters divide over the deity of Christ. The grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, and Ellicott, with the eastern Cyril, hold the Granville Sharp construction: one article governs "God and Saviour," so the phrase calls Christ "our God and Saviour Jesus Christ" — a direct affirmation of his deity (the righteousness of Christ, who is God). A minority reading (reflected in the KJV punctuation) takes "God" as the Father and "our Saviour Jesus Christ" as a distinct person — the righteousness of God [the Father] and of our Saviour. The verse displays the address and the faith through the righteousness of God and our Saviour.

The witnesses agree the substance: Peter, servant and apostle, writes to those given a faith equally precious with the apostles', through the righteousness of God and our Saviour Jesus Christ (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin). But the grammar bears on Christ's deity: Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, and Cyril take the single article (Granville Sharp) to call Christ "our God and Saviour," a proof-text for his deity; the minority reads "God" and "Saviour" as two persons. The verse displays the deity crux. The verse marks the writer: Peter. The verse displays the faith: like precious. The verse displays the debate: one person (Christ is God) or two. The verse displays the righteousness: of God and Saviour. The address displays the precious faith grounded in Christ's righteousness. The verse displays the servant: Peter. The verse displays the faith: shared. The verse displays the Saviour: Jesus Christ. The address and the precious faith through the righteousness of God and our Saviour display the opening and a deity crux: Peter writes to those given a faith equally precious with the apostles', through the righteousness of God and our Saviour Jesus Christ — read by the Granville Sharp construction as calling Christ "our God and Saviour" (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Cyril) or as naming God the Father and the Saviour distinctly. The verse displays the precious faith, multiplied with grace through knowledge (v.2).
2:
"Grace and peace be multiplied unto you through the knowledge of God, and of Jesus our Lord," The brief verse notes the prayer for grace and peace multiplied through knowledge. The grammatical voices note the salutation. Barnes and Robertson note the apostolic greeting: "grace and peace be multiplied (increased, abounding) unto you" — the customary apostolic prayer for grace (God's favour) and peace (the resulting tranquility), here that they be not merely given but multiplied.

On "through the knowledge of God, and of Jesus our Lord," the witnesses note the means. Barnes and Calvin note that grace and peace are multiplied "through the knowledge (epignosis — full, experiential knowledge) of God, and of Jesus our Lord" — the growing knowledge of God and Christ is the channel through which grace and peace increase; knowledge of God is the key theme of the epistle.

On "the knowledge of God, and of Jesus," the grammatical-historical note the centrality: this "knowledge" is not bare information but the deep, personal acquaintance with God and Christ that nourishes the soul; the more one knows God and Jesus, the more grace and peace abound. The verse displays the prayer for grace and peace multiplied through knowledge.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the greeting — "grace and peace be multiplied (increased) unto you," the apostolic prayer for abounding grace and peace (Barnes, Robertson); "through the knowledge (full knowledge) of God, and of Jesus our Lord," knowledge the channel of increasing grace and peace (Barnes, Calvin); the knowledge a deep personal acquaintance nourishing the soul (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the prayer. The verse marks the grace: multiplied. The verse displays the peace: increased. The verse displays the means: knowledge. The verse displays the object: God and Jesus. The salutation displays grace and peace growing through knowledge. The verse displays the grace: prayed. The verse displays the knowledge: of God. The verse displays the Lord: Jesus. The prayer for grace and peace multiplied through knowledge displays the salutation and key theme: grace and peace be multiplied through the growing knowledge of God and of Jesus our Lord — the deep acquaintance with God being the channel of all increase. The verse displays the multiplying knowledge, the source of all things for life and godliness (v.3).
3:
"According as his divine power hath given unto us all things that pertain unto life and godliness, through the knowledge of him that hath called us to glory and virtue:" The witnesses note that God's divine power has given all things for life and godliness. On "his divine power hath given unto us all things that pertain unto life and godliness," the grammatical voices note the full provision. Barnes and Robertson note the gift: "his divine power (the power of God, or of Christ) hath given (granted, bestowed) unto us all things that pertain unto life and godliness" — God's own power has supplied everything needed for spiritual life and godly living; nothing necessary is lacking.

On "all things that pertain unto life and godliness," the witnesses note the sufficiency. Barnes and Calvin note that the provision is complete — "all things" for both "life" (spiritual and eternal life) and "godliness" (piety, holy living); the believer is fully furnished by God's power for all that salvation and holiness require.

On "through the knowledge of him that hath called us to glory and virtue," the grammatical-historical note the means and the call: this provision comes "through the knowledge of him that hath called us to glory and virtue (excellence)" — through the knowledge of God (or Christ), who called us to share his glory and excellence; the divine call is to glory and moral excellence, and knowledge of the Caller is the channel. The verse displays that God's divine power has given all things for life and godliness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the gift — "his divine power hath given... all things that pertain unto life and godliness," God's power supplying everything for spiritual life and godly living (Barnes, Robertson); the provision complete, "all things" for life and godliness, the believer fully furnished (Barnes, Calvin); "through the knowledge of him that hath called us to glory and virtue," the knowledge of the Caller the channel, the call to glory and excellence (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the full provision. The verse marks the power: divine. The verse displays the gift: all things. The verse displays the scope: life and godliness. The verse displays the call: to glory and virtue. The provision displays God's power furnishing all for holy life. The verse displays the power: God's. The verse displays the godliness: provided for. The verse displays the call: to glory. That God's divine power has given all things for life and godliness displays the rich foundation of the Christian life: God's own power has granted everything needed for spiritual life and godliness, through the knowledge of him who called us to glory and excellence. The verse displays the full provision, with its exceeding great promises (v.4).
4:
"Whereby are given unto us exceeding great and precious promises: that by these ye might be partakers of the divine nature, having escaped the corruption that is in the world through lust." The witnesses note the great promises by which we become partakers of the divine nature — the crux of its meaning. On "exceeding great and precious promises," the grammatical voices note the gift of the promises. Barnes and Robertson note that "exceeding great and precious (megista kai timia) promises" are given — the gospel promises, surpassingly great and valuable, the means God uses to make us holy.

On "that by these ye might be partakers of the divine nature," interpreters divide over the meaning. The grammatical-historical Barnes, with the Reformed Calvin, hold that to be "partakers (sharers) of the divine nature (theias physeos)" means to share in God's moral nature — his holiness, purity, and likeness — by the renewal of the image of God in the soul; not a sharing of the divine essence or a becoming God (which would be impossible and pantheistic), but a real participation in God's moral character and life through the new birth. The eastern Cyril (and the patristic tradition) reads it more strongly as theosis — a real participation in the divine life, the believer being truly made a sharer in God by grace, deified (yet without confusion of essence). Both reject any merging of the creature into the divine essence.

On "having escaped the corruption that is in the world through lust," the grammatical-historical note the deliverance: the partaking of the divine nature is joined with "having escaped (fled from) the corruption (moral decay) that is in the world through (in) lust" — sharing God's nature means escaping the world's corrupting desires; the new nature and the flight from corruption go together. The verse displays the great promises by which we become partakers of the divine nature.

The witnesses agree the substance: by the exceeding great and precious promises, believers come to share in the divine nature and escape the world's corruption — the promises being God's means of sanctification (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Cyril). But "partakers of the divine nature" divides them: the Protestant reading takes it as participation in God's moral nature, the renewal of his image and holiness (Barnes, Calvin); the Eastern reads it as theosis, a real participation in the divine life and deification by grace (Cyril) — both denying any sharing of the divine essence. The verse displays the divine-nature crux. The verse marks the promises: exceeding great. The verse displays the debate: moral participation or theosis. The verse displays the partaking: of the divine nature. The verse displays the escape: from corruption. The crux displays the promises making us sharers in God's nature. The verse displays the promises: precious. The verse displays the nature: divine. The verse displays the corruption: escaped. The great promises by which we become partakers of the divine nature display a profound crux: by the exceeding great and precious promises believers are made partakers of the divine nature, escaping the world's corruption — read as participation in God's moral nature and holiness, the renewal of his image (Barnes, Calvin) or as theosis, a real sharing in the divine life by grace (Cyril), both denying any merging of essence. The verse displays the partaken divine nature, pursued by added graces (vv.5-7).
5:
"And beside this, giving all diligence, add to your faith virtue; and to virtue knowledge;" The witnesses note the call to diligently add virtue and knowledge to faith. On "giving all diligence," the grammatical voices note the earnest effort. Barnes and Robertson note the exhortation to active growth: "giving all diligence (bringing in alongside all earnestness, sparing no effort), add to (supply in, furnish abundantly with) your faith virtue" — building on God's provision (vv.3-4), the believer is to put forth every effort to grow in grace; the Christian life requires diligent cultivation.

On "add to your faith virtue," the witnesses note the first addition. Barnes and Henry note the chain of graces beginning with faith: "to your faith (the foundation) add virtue (arete — moral excellence, energy, courage)" — to the faith already possessed, moral excellence and energy are to be added; faith is the root, virtue the first fruit.

On "and to virtue knowledge," the grammatical-historical note the next link: "to virtue knowledge (practical wisdom, discernment)" — to moral excellence is added knowledge, the understanding to direct one's virtue rightly; the graces are linked, each building on the last. The verse displays the call to diligently add virtue and knowledge to faith.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the diligence — "giving all diligence (sparing no effort), add to (supply in) your faith virtue," diligent cultivation building on God's provision (Barnes, Robertson); "to your faith add virtue (moral excellence, energy)," virtue the first fruit on the root of faith (Barnes, Henry); "to virtue knowledge (practical wisdom)," knowledge directing virtue, the graces linked (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the chain of graces. The verse marks the diligence: all. The verse displays the foundation: faith. The verse displays the first addition: virtue. The verse displays the next: knowledge. The exhortation displays graces diligently added to faith. The verse displays the diligence: earnest. The verse displays the virtue: added. The verse displays the knowledge: joined. The call to diligently add virtue and knowledge to faith displays the opening of the ladder of graces: building on God's provision, the believer is to spare no effort to add to his faith moral excellence, and to excellence knowledge. The verse displays the growing graces, continued in temperance, patience, and godliness (v.6).
6:
"And to knowledge temperance; and to temperance patience; and to patience godliness;" The brief verse notes the further graces: temperance, patience, godliness. The grammatical voices note the continuing chain. Barnes and Robertson note the next links: "to knowledge temperance (enkrateia — self-control, mastery of the appetites); and to temperance patience (hypomone — steadfast endurance); and to patience godliness (eusebeia — piety, reverence toward God)" — the ladder continues: knowledge is to be governed by self-control, self-control sustained by patient endurance, and endurance crowned by godliness.

On "temperance... patience," the witnesses note the self-mastery and endurance. Barnes and Calvin note that "temperance (self-control)" restrains the passions and appetites, and "patience (endurance)" bears up under trial; the practical graces that govern the self and sustain it through hardship.

On "patience godliness," the grammatical-historical note the godward grace: "to patience godliness" — endurance is to issue in godliness, true reverence and devotion toward God; the graces move from self-mastery toward piety. The verse displays the further graces: temperance, patience, godliness.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the chain — "to knowledge temperance (self-control); to temperance patience (endurance); to patience godliness (piety)," the ladder of graces continuing (Barnes, Robertson); "temperance" restraining the passions, "patience" bearing up under trial (Barnes, Calvin); "patience godliness," endurance issuing in true reverence toward God (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the graces. The verse marks the temperance: self-control. The verse displays the patience: endurance. The verse displays the godliness: piety. The verse displays the chain: linked. The graces display the soul governed and devoted to God. The verse displays the temperance: added. The verse displays the patience: joined. The verse displays the godliness: crowning. The further graces — temperance, patience, godliness — display the middle rungs of the ladder: to knowledge is added self-control, to self-control patient endurance, and to endurance godliness. The verse displays the growing graces, completed in brotherly kindness and charity (v.7). The deliberate ordering of the chain is itself instructive: knowledge without self-control degenerates into mere speculation, and self-control without patience collapses under the first prolonged trial, while patience that does not ripen into godliness remains a bare stoic endurance rather than a devotion toward God. Each grace both presupposes the last and prepares the next, so that the believer who would reach the summit of love must not leap over the discipline of the self and the endurance of trial that lie between knowledge and piety.
7:
"And to godliness brotherly kindness; and to brotherly kindness charity." The brief verse notes the crowning graces: brotherly kindness and charity. The grammatical voices note the summit of the ladder. Barnes and Robertson note the final links: "to godliness brotherly kindness (philadelphia — love of the brethren); and to brotherly kindness charity (agape — love)" — the chain culminates in love: godliness issues in love for fellow-believers, and brotherly love widens into charity, love for all; love is the crown of the graces.

On "brotherly kindness," the witnesses note the love of the brethren. Barnes and Calvin note that "brotherly kindness" is the warm affection believers have for one another as members of God's family; piety toward God issues in love toward the brethren.

On "charity," the grammatical-historical note the universal love: "charity (agape)" — the highest and most comprehensive grace, love that embraces not only the brethren but all; the love that is the fulfilling of the law and the bond of perfectness; the summit and goal of the whole chain. The verse displays the crowning graces: brotherly kindness and charity.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the summit — "to godliness brotherly kindness (love of the brethren); and to brotherly kindness charity (love)," the chain culminating in love (Barnes, Robertson); "brotherly kindness" the affection for fellow-believers, the fruit of piety (Barnes, Calvin); "charity (agape)" the highest grace, love embracing all, the crown and goal (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the crowning love. The verse marks the brotherly kindness: love of brethren. The verse displays the charity: love of all. The verse displays the summit: love. The verse displays the goal: the bond of perfectness. The graces display love crowning the whole chain. The verse displays the kindness: brotherly. The verse displays the charity: universal. The verse displays the crown: love. The crowning graces — brotherly kindness and charity — display the summit of the ladder: godliness issues in love for the brethren, and brotherly love widens into charity, the universal love that crowns and completes all the graces. The verse displays the crowning charity, whose abounding makes the believer fruitful (v.8).
8:
"For if these things be in you, and abound, they make you that ye shall neither be barren nor unfruitful in the knowledge of our Lord Jesus Christ." The witnesses note that the abounding graces make one fruitful in the knowledge of Christ. On "if these things be in you, and abound," the grammatical voices note the condition. Barnes and Robertson note the requirement: "if these things (the graces just named) be in you, and abound (exist and increase, are present in growing measure)" — not merely present but abounding; the graces must be possessed and growing.

On "they make you that ye shall neither be barren nor unfruitful," the witnesses note the fruitfulness. Barnes and Calvin note the effect: "they make (cause) you that ye shall neither be barren (idle, inactive, argous) nor unfruitful (akarpous — without fruit)" — the abounding graces keep the believer from being idle or fruitless; they produce an active, productive Christian life.

On "in the knowledge of our Lord Jesus Christ," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the sphere: the fruitfulness is "in the knowledge (epignosis — full knowledge) of our Lord Jesus Christ" — the graces make one fruitful in the knowledge of Christ; growth in grace and growth in the knowledge of Christ go together; the knowing is not barren but fruitful. The verse displays that the abounding graces make one fruitful in the knowledge of Christ.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the condition — "if these things be in you, and abound (exist and increase)," the graces present and growing, not merely possessed (Barnes, Robertson); "they make you that ye shall neither be barren nor unfruitful," the abounding graces producing an active, fruitful life (Barnes, Calvin); "in the knowledge of our Lord Jesus Christ," fruitful in the knowledge of Christ, grace and knowledge growing together (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fruitful graces. The verse marks the condition: abounding. The verse displays the effect: not barren. The verse displays the fruit: not unfruitful. The verse displays the sphere: knowledge of Christ. The graces display the believer made fruitful in knowing Christ. The verse displays the things: abounding. The verse displays the barren: avoided. The verse displays the knowledge: fruitful. That the abounding graces make one fruitful in the knowledge of Christ displays the blessed effect of cultivating the graces: if these things are present and growing in the believer, they keep him from being idle or fruitless, making him fruitful in the full knowledge of our Lord Jesus Christ. The verse displays the fruitful knowledge, lacking which one is blind (v.9).
9:
"But he that lacketh these things is blind, and cannot see afar off, and hath forgotten that he was purged from his old sins." The witnesses note the spiritual blindness of those who lack the graces. On "he that lacketh these things is blind, and cannot see afar off," the grammatical voices note the blindness. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast with the fruitful: "he that lacketh these things (the graces) is blind, and cannot see afar off (myopazon — shortsighted, near-sighted)" — the one without the graces is spiritually blind, or at best shortsighted; he cannot see the distant, eternal realities; his spiritual vision is darkened or contracted to the present.

On "blind... cannot see afar off," the witnesses note the dimmed sight. Barnes and Calvin note the two terms: "blind" (wholly without spiritual sight) and "cannot see afar off" (shortsighted, seeing only what is near, the things of this world, not the eternal); the lack of the graces leaves the soul unable to see heavenly things.

On "hath forgotten that he was purged from his old sins," the puritan Henry notes the forgetting: such a one "hath forgotten that he was purged (cleansed) from his old sins" — he has lost sight of his baptismal/conversion cleansing; forgetting the great mercy of his purging from former sins, he fails to live accordingly; ingratitude and forgetfulness mark the unfruitful. The verse displays the spiritual blindness of those who lack the graces.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the blindness — "he that lacketh these things is blind, and cannot see afar off (shortsighted)," the graceless spiritually blind or shortsighted, unable to see eternal realities (Barnes, Robertson); "blind... cannot see afar off," wholly without sight or seeing only the near things of this world (Barnes, Calvin); "hath forgotten that he was purged from his old sins," forgetting his cleansing, ingratitude marking the unfruitful (Henry). The verse displays the blind soul. The verse marks the lack: of the graces. The verse displays the blindness: spiritual. The verse displays the shortsightedness: only the near. The verse displays the forgetting: his cleansing. The blindness displays the graceless soul unable to see the eternal. The verse displays the blind: graceless. The verse displays the sight: contracted. The verse displays the purging: forgotten. The spiritual blindness of those who lack the graces displays the dark contrast to the fruitful: the one without these graces is blind, or shortsighted — unable to see the eternal — and has forgotten that he was cleansed from his old sins. The verse displays the forgetful blindness, against which diligence makes the calling sure (v.10).
10:
"Wherefore the rather, brethren, give diligence to make your calling and election sure: for if ye do these things, ye shall never fall:" The witnesses note the call to make one's calling and election sure by diligence. On "give diligence to make your calling and election sure," interpreters note how election is "made sure." The grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson, with the Reformed Calvin, hold that the believer makes his calling and election "sure (bebaian — firm, confirmed)" not by altering God's eternal decree (which is unchangeable) but by confirming it to himself — giving evidence, through the diligent practice of the graces, that he is truly called and elect; the good works are the proof and assurance of election, not its cause. The election is sure in God; diligence makes it sure (certain, evident) to the believer's own conscience.

On "give diligence," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and Clarke note the earnest effort: the believer is to be diligent, working out his salvation; the exhortation presses the necessity of perseverance and active obedience to confirm one's standing.

On "if ye do these things, ye shall never fall," the witnesses note the promise. Barnes and Calvin note the assurance: "if ye do these things (practice the graces), ye shall never fall (stumble, apostatize)" — diligent practice of the graces preserves from falling; the one who grows in grace is kept secure; perseverance is the fruit and evidence of true election. The verse displays the call to make one's calling and election sure by diligence.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree the substance: by diligent practice of the graces the believer confirms and gives evidence of his calling and election, and is preserved from falling (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley). The Reformed and grammatical voices stress that diligence makes election sure to the believer (assurance), not altering God's decree; the Arminian voices stress the necessity of perseverance. The verse displays the assured calling. The verse marks the diligence: to be given. The verse displays the calling: made sure. The verse displays the election: confirmed. The verse displays the promise: never fall. The exhortation displays diligence confirming election and preserving from falling. The verse displays the calling: sure. The verse displays the election: evidenced. The verse displays the falling: prevented. The call to make one's calling and election sure by diligence displays the practical issue of the graces: the believer is to give diligence to confirm his calling and election — making it sure to himself by the evidence of the graces — for if he practices these things he shall never fall. The verse displays the sure calling, opening the abundant entrance into the kingdom (v.11).
11:
"For so an entrance shall be ministered unto you abundantly into the everlasting kingdom of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ." The brief verse notes the abundant entrance into the everlasting kingdom. The grammatical voices note the rich reward. Barnes and Robertson note the promise that crowns diligence: "so an entrance (entry, admission) shall be ministered (richly supplied, furnished) unto you abundantly (richly, with full measure) into the everlasting kingdom of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ" — to the diligent believer a rich, abundant entrance into the eternal kingdom is granted; not a bare admission but a triumphant, abundant entry.

On "ministered... abundantly," the witnesses note the richness. Barnes and Calvin note that the entrance is "ministered abundantly" — supplied richly, as a victor welcomed in triumph; the diligent, fruitful Christian enters heaven not barely but gloriously, with full assurance and welcome.

On "the everlasting kingdom of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ," the grammatical-historical note the kingdom and the title: the entrance is into "the everlasting (eternal) kingdom of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ" — Christ's eternal kingdom; and the title "our Lord and Saviour" (echoing v.1) again ascribes lordship and saving glory to Christ. The verse displays the abundant entrance into the everlasting kingdom.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the reward — "an entrance shall be ministered (richly supplied) unto you abundantly into the everlasting kingdom," a rich, abundant entry granted the diligent (Barnes, Robertson); "ministered abundantly," supplied richly as a victor welcomed in triumph, entering not barely but gloriously (Barnes, Calvin); "the everlasting kingdom of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ," Christ's eternal kingdom, the title ascribing him lordship and saving glory (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the abundant entrance. The verse marks the entrance: abundant. The verse displays the ministering: rich. The verse displays the kingdom: everlasting. The verse displays the Lord: Saviour Jesus Christ. The reward displays the diligent richly welcomed into the eternal kingdom. The verse displays the entrance: granted. The verse displays the kingdom: eternal. The verse displays the Saviour: Christ. The abundant entrance into the everlasting kingdom displays the rich reward of diligence: to the fruitful believer a triumphant, abundant entry shall be richly supplied into the everlasting kingdom of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ. The verse displays the abundant entrance, the goal of the remembered truths (v.12).
12:
"Wherefore I will not be negligent to put you always in remembrance of these things, though ye know them, and be established in the present truth." The witnesses note Peter's resolve to keep reminding them, though they know the truth. On "I will not be negligent to put you always in remembrance of these things," the grammatical voices note the apostle's diligence. Barnes and Robertson note Peter's pastoral resolve: "I will not be negligent (I will not neglect, I will always be ready) to put you always in remembrance (to remind you continually) of these things" — Peter purposes to keep reminding them of these truths; the duty of repeated reminder, even of known truths.

On "though ye know them, and be established in the present truth," the witnesses note the readers' standing. Barnes and Calvin note the acknowledgment: "though ye know them (already), and be established (firmly settled, grounded) in the present truth (the truth now possessed, the gospel)" — the readers already know and are grounded in the truth; yet reminder is still needful, for even the established need to be stirred up and confirmed.

On the duty of reminder, the puritan Henry notes the value: the most established believers benefit from being reminded of known truths; memory is weak and the heart prone to forget; faithful repetition guards against decline. The verse displays Peter's resolve to keep reminding them.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the resolve — "I will not be negligent to put you always in remembrance of these things," Peter purposing to remind them continually (Barnes, Robertson); "though ye know them, and be established in the present truth," the readers already grounded, yet reminder still needful (Barnes, Calvin); the established benefiting from reminder, memory weak (Henry). The verse displays the faithful reminder. The verse marks the resolve: not negligent. The verse displays the duty: always remind. The verse displays the readers: knowing, established. The verse displays the need: reminder still. The resolve displays Peter reminding even the grounded. The verse displays the remembrance: pressed. The verse displays the truth: present. The verse displays the established: still reminded. Peter's resolve to keep reminding them, though they know the truth, displays the apostle's pastoral diligence: he will not neglect to remind them continually of these things — for even those who know and are established in the truth need to be stirred up by remembrance. The verse displays the faithful reminder, urgent because his death is near (vv.13-14).
13:
"Yea, I think it meet, as long as I am in this tabernacle, to stir you up by putting you in remembrance;" The brief verse notes Peter's duty to stir them up while he lives. The grammatical voices note the figure of the body. Barnes and Robertson note the apostle's sense of duty: "I think it meet (right, fitting), as long as I am in this tabernacle (skenoma — tent, the body as the soul's temporary dwelling), to stir you up (arouse, awaken) by putting you in remembrance (by way of reminder)" — Peter considers it his duty, while he yet lives in the body, to keep arousing them by reminder.

On "this tabernacle," the witnesses note the body as a tent. Barnes and Calvin note that "tabernacle (tent)" pictures the body as a temporary, movable dwelling for the soul — fittingly used by one soon to "put it off" (v.14); the figure of life's brevity.

On "stir you up by putting you in remembrance," the puritan Henry notes the rousing: to "stir you up" is to awaken and energize their minds, lest they grow drowsy or forgetful; reminder is not idle repetition but a rousing of the soul to alertness and diligence. The verse displays Peter's duty to stir them up while he lives.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the duty — "I think it meet (fitting), as long as I am in this tabernacle (the body), to stir you up (arouse) by putting you in remembrance," Peter's duty while he lives to rouse them by reminder (Barnes, Robertson); "this tabernacle (tent)" the body as a temporary dwelling, life brief (Barnes, Calvin); "stir you up," awakening their minds lest they grow drowsy, reminder a rousing (Henry). The verse displays the rousing reminder. The verse marks the duty: meet. The verse displays the body: this tabernacle. The verse displays the action: stir up. The verse displays the means: remembrance. The duty displays Peter rousing them while he lives. The verse displays the tabernacle: temporary. The verse displays the stirring: of the soul. The verse displays the remembrance: rousing. Peter's duty to stir them up while he lives displays the urgency of his reminders: he considers it fitting, as long as he remains in the tent of the body, to arouse them by putting them in remembrance — rousing their souls to alertness. The verse displays the stirring reminder, pressed by the nearness of his death (v.14).
14:
"Knowing that shortly I must put off this my tabernacle, even as our Lord Jesus Christ hath shewed me." The witnesses note Peter's awareness of his approaching death, as Christ foretold. On "knowing that shortly I must put off this my tabernacle," the grammatical voices note the foreknowledge. Barnes and Robertson note Peter's certainty of his nearing death: "knowing that shortly (soon, swiftly) I must put off (lay aside, as one removes a tent or garment) this my tabernacle (the body)" — Peter knows his death is near; to die is to "put off" the tent of the body; the calm certainty of one facing the end.

On "put off this my tabernacle," the witnesses note the figure. Barnes and Calvin note that death is here pictured as putting off a tent — a temporary removal of the body, the soul laying aside its dwelling; the figure softens death into a mere change of abode for the believer.

On "even as our Lord Jesus Christ hath shewed me," the eastern and grammatical-historical note the revelation: Peter knows this "even as our Lord Jesus Christ hath shewed (made clear, signified) me" — Christ had foretold Peter's death (John 21:18-19), and perhaps had recently signified its nearness; Peter's foreknowledge rests on Christ's own word. The verse displays Peter's awareness of his approaching death, as Christ foretold.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the foreknowledge — "knowing that shortly I must put off (lay aside) this my tabernacle (the body)," Peter certain his death is near, death a putting off of the tent (Barnes, Robertson); death pictured as putting off a tent, the soul laying aside its dwelling, a change of abode (Barnes, Calvin); "even as our Lord Jesus Christ hath shewed me," Christ having foretold Peter's death (John 21:18-19), his foreknowledge resting on Christ's word (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the nearing death. The verse marks the knowledge: of death. The verse displays the figure: put off the tent. The verse displays the nearness: shortly. The verse displays the source: Christ foretold. The awareness displays Peter calmly facing the end Christ foretold. The verse displays the tabernacle: put off. The verse displays the death: near. The verse displays the Lord: foretold it. Peter's awareness of his approaching death, as Christ foretold, displays the urgency behind his reminders: knowing that he must soon put off the tent of the body, as the Lord Jesus had shown him, he is the more earnest to leave them grounded. The verse displays the nearing death, prompting a lasting remembrance (v.15).
15:
"Moreover I will endeavour that ye may be able after my decease to have these things always in remembrance." The brief verse notes Peter's care to provide a lasting remembrance after his death. The grammatical voices note the lasting provision. Barnes and Robertson note Peter's intention: "I will endeavour (be diligent, make every effort) that ye may be able after my decease (exodos — departure, death) to have these things always in remembrance (to call them to mind continually)" — Peter purposes to provide a means by which, even after he is gone, they may continually remember these truths.

On "after my decease," the witnesses note the word for death. Barnes and Calvin note that "decease (exodos — departure, exodus)" is a gentle term for death — a going-out, a departure (the same word used of Christ's death at the Transfiguration, Luke 9:31); death is the soul's departure, not its end.

On "have these things always in remembrance," the grammatical-historical note the enduring witness: Peter desires a lasting remembrance — many take this as a reference to his providing a written record (this epistle, or the Gospel of Mark, traditionally Peter's testimony); his concern is that the truth outlive him and be continually available. The verse displays Peter's care to provide a lasting remembrance after his death.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the provision — "I will endeavour (be diligent) that ye may be able after my decease to have these things always in remembrance," Peter providing a means for continual remembrance after he is gone (Barnes, Robertson); "decease (exodos — departure)" a gentle term for death, the soul's going-out (Luke 9:31, Barnes, Calvin); a lasting written remembrance, the truth outliving him (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the lasting remembrance. The verse marks the endeavour: diligent. The verse displays the decease: departure. The verse displays the aim: always in remembrance. The verse displays the means: enduring record. The care displays Peter ensuring the truth outlives him. The verse displays the decease: Peter's. The verse displays the remembrance: lasting. The verse displays the things: preserved. Peter's care to provide a lasting remembrance after his death displays his final pastoral concern: he will make every effort that, even after his departure, they may always be able to call these truths to mind — the truth secured beyond his lifetime. The verse displays the lasting remembrance, resting on no fable but eyewitness testimony (v.16).
16:
"For we have not followed cunningly devised fables, when we made known unto you the power and coming of our Lord Jesus Christ, but were eyewitnesses of his majesty." The witnesses note that the apostles preached no fable but were eyewitnesses of Christ's majesty. On "we have not followed cunningly devised fables," the grammatical voices note the denial. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast: "we have not followed (sesophismenois mythois) cunningly devised (cleverly invented, sophisticated) fables (myths), when we made known unto you the power and coming (parousia) of our Lord Jesus Christ" — the apostolic message about Christ's power and (second) coming is no cleverly invented myth, unlike the fables of false teachers and pagan religion.

On "the power and coming of our Lord Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the subject. Barnes and Calvin note that "the power and coming" refers to Christ's majesty and his coming (his first advent in glory, or especially his second coming in power) — the great themes the apostles proclaimed, grounded in fact, not fancy.

On "but were eyewitnesses of his majesty," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the testimony: the apostles "were eyewitnesses (epoptai — spectators, those who saw with their own eyes) of his majesty (megaleiotetos)" — Peter and the others personally witnessed Christ's majesty, especially at the Transfiguration (vv.17-18); the gospel rests on eyewitness testimony, not invented tales. The verse displays that the apostles preached no fable but were eyewitnesses of Christ's majesty.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the denial — "we have not followed cunningly devised (cleverly invented) fables (myths)... the power and coming of our Lord Jesus Christ," the message no invented myth (Barnes, Robertson); "the power and coming," Christ's majesty and his coming in power, grounded in fact (Barnes, Calvin); "were eyewitnesses of his majesty," the apostles personally witnessing Christ's majesty (the Transfiguration), the gospel resting on eyewitness testimony (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the eyewitness gospel. The verse marks the denial: no fables. The verse displays the subject: power and coming. The verse displays the testimony: eyewitnesses. The verse displays the majesty: seen. The denial displays the gospel grounded in eyewitness fact. The verse displays the fables: rejected. The verse displays the coming: proclaimed. The verse displays the majesty: witnessed. That the apostles preached no fable but were eyewitnesses of Christ's majesty displays the firm ground of the gospel: the message of Christ's power and coming is no cleverly invented myth, for the apostles were eyewitnesses of his majesty. The verse displays the eyewitness testimony, confirmed by the voice on the mount (vv.17-18).
17:
"For he received from God the Father honour and glory, when there came such a voice to him from the excellent glory, This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased." The witnesses note the honour and glory Christ received from the Father at the Transfiguration. On "he received from God the Father honour and glory," the grammatical voices note the divine bestowal. Barnes and Robertson note the event recalled: "he received from God the Father honour and glory" — at the Transfiguration, Christ received honour and glory from the Father; the Father publicly honoured the Son before the eyewitness apostles.

On "when there came such a voice to him from the excellent glory," the witnesses note the divine voice. Barnes and Calvin note the source: "when there came such a voice to him from the excellent glory (the magnificent glory, the bright cloud, the Shekinah, representing the Father's presence)" — a voice came from the glorious cloud, the Father himself speaking; the "excellent glory" is the visible token of God's majestic presence.

On "This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the testimony: the voice declared, "This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased" — the Father's own attestation of Jesus as his beloved Son, the object of his good pleasure; the divine witness to Christ's sonship, heard by the apostles. The verse displays the honour and glory Christ received from the Father at the Transfiguration.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the bestowal — "he received from God the Father honour and glory," Christ honoured by the Father at the Transfiguration before the apostles (Barnes, Robertson); "a voice... from the excellent glory," the Father speaking from the glorious cloud, the token of his presence (Barnes, Calvin); "This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased," the Father's attestation of Jesus' sonship (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the Father's witness. The verse marks the honour: from the Father. The verse displays the voice: from the glory. The verse displays the declaration: my beloved Son. The verse displays the pleasure: well pleased. The event displays the Father honouring the Son before witnesses. The verse displays the glory: received. The verse displays the voice: divine. The verse displays the Son: beloved. The honour and glory Christ received from the Father at the Transfiguration display the supreme confirmation of the eyewitness gospel: Christ received honour and glory from God the Father when the voice came from the excellent glory, "This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased." The verse displays the Father's testimony, heard by the apostles on the mount (v.18).
18:
"And this voice which came from heaven we heard, when we were with him in the holy mount." The brief verse notes the apostles' hearing the voice on the holy mount. The grammatical voices note the eyewitness confirmation. Barnes and Robertson note the personal testimony: "this voice which came from heaven we heard, when we were with him in the holy mount" — Peter (with James and John) personally heard the Father's voice from heaven at the Transfiguration; the apostle bears firsthand witness.

On "we heard," the witnesses note the firsthand testimony. Barnes and Calvin note the emphasis on "we heard" — not report but personal hearing; the apostles' testimony to Christ rests on what they themselves heard and saw, the strongest kind of evidence.

On "in the holy mount," the eastern Cyril notes the hallowed place: "the holy mount" — the mount of Transfiguration, called "holy" because of the divine manifestation there; the place was sanctified by the presence and voice of God; the apostles stood where God revealed his Son. The verse displays the apostles' hearing the voice on the holy mount.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the testimony — "this voice which came from heaven we heard, when we were with him in the holy mount," Peter personally hearing the Father's voice at the Transfiguration (Barnes, Robertson); "we heard," firsthand personal hearing, the strongest evidence (Barnes, Calvin); "the holy mount," the mount sanctified by the divine manifestation (Cyril). The verse displays the heard voice. The verse marks the voice: from heaven. The verse displays the hearing: personal. The verse displays the place: the holy mount. The verse displays the witness: firsthand. The testimony displays the apostles hearing God's voice on the mount. The verse displays the voice: heard. The verse displays the mount: holy. The verse displays the witness: eyewitness. The apostles' hearing the voice on the holy mount displays the firsthand ground of their testimony: the voice from heaven they themselves heard, being with Christ on the holy mount of Transfiguration — the strongest evidence for the gospel they preached. The verse displays the heard testimony, confirming the still surer word of prophecy (v.19).
19:
"We have also a more sure word of prophecy; whereunto ye do well that ye take heed, as unto a light that shineth in a dark place, until the day dawn, and the day star arise in your hearts:" The witnesses note the sure word of prophecy as a lamp in the dark. On "we have also a more sure word of prophecy," interpreters note the comparison. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note two readings: "we have also a more sure (bebaioteron — firmer, more confirmed) word of prophecy" — either that the prophetic word is "more sure" (even than the voice on the mount, the written prophecies confirmed by their fulfillment and standing as an abiding witness), or, comparatively, that the prophetic word is now made more sure/confirmed by the Transfiguration the apostles witnessed; the Old Testament prophecy and the apostolic eyewitness mutually confirm Christ.

On "ye do well that ye take heed, as unto a light that shineth in a dark place," the witnesses note the lamp. Barnes and Calvin note the figure: "ye do well that ye take heed (give attention), as unto a light (lamp) that shineth in a dark (murky, squalid) place" — the prophetic word is a lamp shining in the darkness of this world; believers do well to heed it, for it gives light amid the gloom until full day.

On "until the day dawn, and the day star arise in your hearts," the grammatical-historical note the dawn: the lamp serves "until the day dawn (the full light of day breaks), and the day star (phosphoros — the morning star) arise in your hearts" — the prophetic word guides until the day of full revelation dawns and Christ, the morning star, rises in the heart; interpreters take this of the fuller light of the gospel, or of Christ's coming, or of the inward illumination that completes what prophecy began. The verse displays the sure word of prophecy as a lamp in the dark.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the prophetic word — "we have also a more sure (firmer) word of prophecy," the prophetic word sure and confirmed, mutually attesting Christ with the eyewitness testimony (Barnes, Robertson); "a light (lamp) that shineth in a dark place," the word a lamp in the world's darkness, to be heeded (Barnes, Calvin); "until the day dawn, and the day star arise in your hearts," the lamp guiding until full day and Christ the morning star rises within (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the prophetic lamp. The verse marks the word: more sure. The verse displays the heeding: do well. The verse displays the figure: lamp in darkness. The verse displays the dawn: day star in the heart. The word displays prophecy guiding until the full day of Christ. The verse displays the prophecy: sure. The verse displays the light: shining. The verse displays the day star: arising. The sure word of prophecy as a lamp in the dark displays the abiding guide of the believer: the firm word of prophecy, to which they do well to take heed, is a lamp shining in this dark world until the day dawns and the morning star, Christ, arises in their hearts. The verse displays the prophetic lamp, whose origin is not human (v.20).
20:
"Knowing this first, that no prophecy of the scripture is of any private interpretation." The witnesses note that no prophecy of Scripture is of private interpretation — a major crux. On the meaning of "private interpretation," interpreters divide. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson, with the Reformed Calvin and the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and Clarke, hold the dominant Protestant reading: "no prophecy of the scripture is of any private interpretation (idias epilyseos — its own disclosure, origination, or solving)" means that no prophecy originated from the prophet's own mind or private impulse — it was not the prophet's own invention or disclosure but came from God (as v.21 explains: the prophets "spake as they were moved by the Holy Ghost"). Barnes: the truths "were not originated by themselves... but were of higher origin"; Clarke: "not by the mere private impulse of his own mind"; Wesley: "it is not any man's own word." The next verse (the explanatory "for") settles this sense.

The Roman Catholic reading — that Scripture is not to be privately interpreted by individuals but only by the Church's authority — is named and firmly rejected by the Protestant voices. Calvin: "the Papists are doubly foolish" to conclude that no private interpretation is authoritative, claiming for their councils the chief right of interpreting Scripture; Peter does not prohibit each one from interpreting, but shows that whatever men bring "of their own" is profane, the word being set in opposition to divine revelation. Barnes: the text says nothing about the Church's authority, is confined to prophecy, states a fact (not a duty), and cannot be used to bar the people from free access to Scripture.

The witnesses agree the substance (with v.21): prophecy did not originate in the prophet's own private mind but came from God by the Holy Spirit — therefore to be revered as the very oracles of God. The Protestant voices unite in reading "private interpretation" as private origination/disclosure (not the prophet's own invention), and in rejecting the Catholic use of the text to deny individuals the right to interpret Scripture. The verse displays the private-interpretation crux. The verse marks the prophecy: of Scripture. The verse displays the debate: private origination, or the Church's sole authority. The verse displays the Protestant reading: not the prophet's own invention. The verse displays the rejection: of the Catholic reading. The crux displays prophecy as not of human origin. The verse displays the Scripture: prophetic. The verse displays the interpretation: not private. The verse displays the origin: divine. That no prophecy of Scripture is of private interpretation displays a major Protestant/Catholic crux: read by the Protestant consensus as meaning prophecy did not originate from the prophet's own private mind or impulse but from God (Barnes, Calvin, Wesley, Clarke), against the Roman Catholic reading that Scripture must be interpreted only by the Church's authority (named and rejected). The verse displays the divinely-originated prophecy, moved by the Holy Ghost (v.21).
21:
"For the prophecy came not in old time by the will of man: but holy men of God spake as they were moved by the Holy Ghost." The witnesses note that prophecy came not by man's will but by the Holy Spirit's moving — the great inspiration text. On "the prophecy came not in old time by the will of man," the grammatical voices note the negative. Barnes and Robertson note the denial of human origin: "the prophecy came not in old time (anciently) by the will of man (not at man's initiative, not from human choice or invention)" — prophecy did not arise from the prophet's own will or decision; it was not a human production; this explains v.20.

On "holy men of God spake," the witnesses note the human instruments. Barnes and Calvin note that "holy men of God (godly men, consecrated to God) spake" — the prophets were the human agents, holy men who uttered the prophecies; the message came through men, but not from them.

On "as they were moved by the Holy Ghost," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the inspiration: they spoke "as they were moved (pheromenoi — borne along, carried) by the Holy Ghost" — the Holy Spirit bore the prophets along, as wind drives a ship; the words they spoke were given and impelled by the Spirit; the classic statement of the divine inspiration of Scripture — God the author, holy men the instruments. The verse displays that prophecy came not by man's will but by the Holy Spirit's moving.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the denial — "the prophecy came not... by the will of man," prophecy not arising from human will or invention (Barnes, Robertson); "holy men of God spake," the prophets the human agents through whom (not from whom) the message came (Barnes, Calvin); "as they were moved (borne along) by the Holy Ghost," the Spirit carrying the prophets as wind drives a ship, the classic statement of inspiration (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the inspired prophecy. The verse marks the negative: not by man's will. The verse displays the agents: holy men. The verse displays the moving: by the Holy Ghost. The verse displays the doctrine: divine inspiration. The verse displays prophecy as the Spirit's word through holy men. The verse displays the will: not man's. The verse displays the men: holy. The verse displays the Spirit: moving. That prophecy came not by man's will but by the Holy Spirit's moving displays the great ground of Scripture's authority: prophecy did not originate in human will, but holy men of God spoke as they were borne along by the Holy Ghost — the divine inspiration that makes the prophetic word the very oracle of God. The verse displays the Spirit-moved prophecy, closing the chapter and grounding the warning against false teachers (ch.2).
